_meta.authoringNotes: ICD-10 K91.83. Content reviewed against ACG Clinical Guideline: Management of Pouchitis 2024, Consensus Guidelines on Pouchitis (IOIBD 2022), and UpToDate Lexicomp. Pouchitis Disease Activity Index (PDAI) thresholds and antibiotic response rates cross-verified against published cohort studies.

h1Title: Pouchitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Pouchitis is acute or chronic inflammation of an ileal pouch-anal anastomosis constructed surgically after colectomy for ulcerative colitis, the most common indication. Between 40 and 50 percent of patients with an IPAA will experience at least one episode of pouchitis during their lifetime, and 10 to 15 percent develop chronic antibiotic-refractory disease that significantly impairs quality of life. The condition presents with increased stool frequency, urgency, tenesmus, nocturnal seepage, and pelvic cramping — symptoms that overlap with the original ulcerative colitis but typically respond to antibiotic therapy. Diagnosis is confirmed using the Pouchitis Disease Activity Index scoring clinical symptoms alongside endoscopic and histologic findings. Most episodes respond to a 14-day course of metronidazole or ciprofloxacin.

keyStats.prevalence: 40-50% of IPAA patients experience at least one pouchitis episode; 10-15% develop chronic antibiotic-refractory disease
keyStats.demographics: Occurs after IPAA surgery for ulcerative colitis; median onset 1-3 years post-surgery
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of first pouchitis: 30-40 years; most patients are in their 20s-40s at the time of pouch surgery
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 200,000-300,000 IPAA pouches in use worldwide; pouchitis is the most common long-term complication of IPAA

definition: Pouchitis (ICD-10: K91.83) is inflammation of an ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA), the most common surgical procedure performed for ulcerative colitis that requires colectomy. The IPAA is constructed by creating a J-shaped pouch from the terminal ileum and connecting it to the anal canal, preserving bowel continuity and avoiding a permanent ileostomy. Pouchitis refers specifically to inflammation of the pouch reservoir — not irritation of the perianal skin, cuffitis (inflammation of the retained rectal cuff), or pouch outlet obstruction. The condition exists on a spectrum from acute, antibiotic-responsive episodes to chronic antibiotic-refractory disease that significantly impairs quality of life. The pathophysiology is multifactorial and incompletely understood, but current evidence points to a dysregulated immune response to altered luminal bacteria in a genetically susceptible host. Stool stasis in the pouch promotes bacterial overgrowth and fermentation of short-chain fatty acids, which are normally abundant in the colon and important for colonocyte health. Loss of the colon — the organ that normally absorbs short-chain fatty acids — leaves the pouch epithelium deprived of its primary energy source. The resulting mucosal inflammation in some patients is treated effectively with antibiotics, while in others it persists and becomes refractory.

typesClassification.description: A single or infrequent episodes of pouch inflammation, typically responding to a 14-day course of antibiotics. Characterized by acute symptoms (increased stool frequency, urgency, cramping) that resolve completely with treatment. Most patients with acute pouchitis return to their baseline pouch function after treatment.

Recurrent episodes of pouchitis (3 or more per year) that consistently respond to antibiotic treatment. Requires ongoing surveillance and intermittent courses of antibiotics. Does not cause structural damage but significantly impacts quality of life through repeated symptom flares.

Pouchitis that fails to respond adequately to standard antibiotic regimens — typically ciprofloxacin and/or metronidazole. This is the most challenging form, associated with significant morbidity, pouch failure, and potential surgical revision. Requires escalation to immunomodulators, anti-TNF therapy, or surgical management.

Inflammation of the retained rectal mucosal cuff above the anastomosis. Often coexists with pouchitis but is a distinct entity. Presents with rectal bleeding and urgency; treated with topical corticosteroids or 5-ASA. Frequently misdiagnosed as pouchitis.

Mechanical narrowing of the pouch inlet, outlet, or channel causing obstructive symptoms — difficulty evacuating, incomplete emptying, abdominal cramping. May mimic pouchitis symptoms. Treated with endoscopic balloon dilation or surgical revision.

primarySymptoms: Increased stool frequency — the most common symptom; patients typically report 6-10 or more BMs per day (normal pouch function is 4-6 BMs daily).
Urgency — a sudden, compelling need to have a bowel movement, often with inadequate warning time.
Nocturnal seepage — incontinence at night, with the patient waking to find soiling. This is a particularly troubling symptom for quality of life.
Tenesmus — a sensation of incomplete evacuation and persistent rectal fullness, even after defecation.
Lower abdominal or pelvic cramping — cramping pain associated with defecation or independent of it.
Bloody stools — bright red blood mixed with stool or coating the surface; indicates active mucosal inflammation.
Increased mucus in stools — gelatinous mucus coating the stool surface, produced by inflamed pouch mucosa.
Fever and malaise — in more severe acute episodes, systemic symptoms may accompany the GI symptoms.
Joint pains (extraintestinal manifestation) — arthralgias affecting large joints can accompany active pouchitis, particularly in patients with spondyloarthropathy history.
Erythema nodosum or pyoderma gangrenosum — extraintestinal skin manifestations may appear during pouchitis flares, particularly in patients with a prior history of these conditions.
earlyWarningSigns: An increase in daily bowel movements beyond the established baseline — even 1-2 extra BMs per day can signal an early pouchitis episode.
New urgency or a shortened warning time before defecation.
The return of nocturnal stooling in a patient who had been sleeping through the night — a sensitive early sign.
Increased mucus production — a change from normal to excessive mucus coating the stool.
Mild lower abdominal cramping or a sense of pouch 'fullness' — particularly after meals, suggesting early inflammation.
emergencySigns: High fever above 39 degrees Celsius with severe pouch symptoms — possible septic complication of the pouch or concurrent toxic megacolon of the pouch.
Profuse, continuous rectal bleeding — requires urgent pouchoscopy and exclusion of pouch ulcerations or Crohn's disease of the pouch.
Signs of sepsis (tachycardia, hypotension, altered mental status) — possible perforation or abscess requiring urgent surgical review.
Severe abdominal distension with inability to pass stool or flatus — possible bowel obstruction or pouch outlet obstruction.
Acute severe perianal pain with fever — possible perianal abscess or fistula requiring urgent surgical intervention.

causes.cause: Fecal stasis and bacterial overgrowth in the pouch
causes.description: The ileal pouch, unlike the colon, is not designed for long-term fecal storage. Stasis of intestinal contents promotes bacterial overgrowth, particularly anaerobes that ferment bile acids and consume short-chain fatty acids. This changes the luminal environment from one that supports colonocyte health to one that drives inflammation.

The pouch mucosa in pouchitis patients shows increased infiltration of CD4+ T lymphocytes, neutrophils, and mast cells. Cytokine profiling reveals a Th1/Th17 polarization similar to inflammatory bowel disease — elevated TNF-alpha, IL-1 beta, IL-17, and interferon-gamma. This suggests the inflammation is driven by immune dysregulation rather than simple bacterial invasion.

causes.cause: Loss of short-chain fatty acid-producing colonic microbiota
causes.description: The colon harbors a complex microbiome that ferments dietary fiber to short-chain fatty acids (butyrate, propionate, acetate), which are the primary energy source for colonocytes and have anti-inflammatory properties. After colectomy, this microbial community and its metabolic products are absent in the pouch, potentially contributing to epithelial dysfunction.

Variants in NOD2 (associated with Crohn's disease susceptibility) are overrepresented in patients with chronic antibiotic-refractory pouchitis. Other polymorphisms in genes involved in innate immune sensing (TLR4, IRGM) and mucosal barrier function (MUC genes) are also implicated. This suggests that some patients have a genetic predisposition to dysregulated immune responses to pouch bacteria.

causes.cause: Misdiagnosis of Crohn's disease as ulcerative colitis
causes.description: A subset of patients labeled as having ulcerative colitis who undergo IPAA actually have Crohn's disease — either because the Crohn's features (skip lesions, granulomas, deep fistulae) were not apparent pre-operatively or were not sought. Crohn's disease of the pouch behaves differently and is often refractory to standard pouchitis treatment. This is the most important differential to establish before attributing symptoms to pouchitis.

In patients with long-standing pouches or those with compromised mesenteric blood supply (from repeated surgeries, radiation, or vascular disease), chronic low-grade ischemia can contribute to mucosal inflammation and ulceration, producing a picture that mimics pouchitis. This is distinguished by characteristic endoscopic findings of ischemic change.

riskFactors.description: Patients with total colitis have higher rates of pouchitis than those with limited disease. The extent of colonic inflammation before colectomy correlates with post-operative pouch inflammation risk.
Patients with PSC and IPAA have a 2-3 fold higher risk of pouchitis than UC-only patients. The reasons are unclear but may involve altered bile acid metabolism and bacterial composition.

Backwash ileitis (inflammation of the terminal ileum due to colonic backflow) before surgery is associated with higher pouchitis risk, possibly because it reflects a more extensive inflammatory phenotype.
NOD2 variants — strongly associated with Crohn's disease — are overrepresented in chronic antibiotic-refractory pouchitis. Genetic testing may help identify high-risk patients.
Interestingly, non-smokers have higher pouchitis rates than smokers — opposite to the pattern in IBD generally. Nicotine may have a protective anti-inflammatory effect on the pouch mucosa.
Patients with higher stool frequency and more severe UC symptoms before surgery have higher pouchitis rates — possibly reflecting a more severe underlying inflammatory phenotype.
Larger pouches may have more stool stasis and bacterial overgrowth, increasing pouchitis risk. Pouch configuration at surgery influences this.
Mechanical narrowing at the anastomotic site causes fecal stasis upstream, promoting bacterial overgrowth and pouchitis. Strictures are often treated with endoscopic dilation.
Repeated courses of broad-spectrum antibiotics can disrupt the pouch microbiome, potentially reducing protective species and promoting overgrowth of inflammatory bacteria.
Pouchitis episodes cluster in spring and fall in temperate climates — possibly related to environmental triggers (allergens, infections) that exacerbate mucosal immune responses.

affectedDemographics: IPAA surgery is performed almost exclusively for ulcerative colitis (95% of cases); the remaining cases are for familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) or indeterminate colitis
Pouchitis does not occur in patients who had IPAA for FAP — since the colon is normal, not inflamed — confirming that the underlying UC drives the inflammation
Median time to first pouchitis episode is 1-3 years after surgery; episodes can occur as early as weeks post-surgery or decades later
Women and men have equal pouchitis risk; the condition does not vary by sex
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of pouchitis requires the combination of compatible clinical symptoms, endoscopic evidence of inflammation, and histologic confirmation. The Pouchitis Disease Activity Index (PDAI) is the validated tool that integrates all three domains — clinical symptoms (stool frequency, urgency, cramping, mucus, fever), endoscopic findings (edema, granularity, friability, erosions, ulcers), and histology (polymorphonuclear infiltration of the lamina propria). A PDAI score of 7 or above confirms pouchitis. Pouchoscopy (direct visualization of the pouch with a flexible endoscope) is the cornerstone of diagnosis — it confirms mucosal inflammation, allows exclusion of mechanical problems (strictures, retained rectal staple lines), allows biopsy for histology, and allows assessment of the anal transitional zone (cuff) for cuffitis. Flexible sigmoidoscopy is adequate in patients with a short cuff. CT or MRI enterography is used when a peripouch abscess, fistula, or surgical complication is suspected. Laboratory tests are supportive: fecal calprotectin is markedly elevated in pouchitis (often >200 µg/g, versus <50 µg/g in a healthy pouch) and is useful for monitoring. CRP may be elevated in severe or systemic episodes. Routine blood tests help assess nutritional status and exclude concurrent conditions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for diagnosis. Direct visualization confirms mucosal inflammation and allows targeted biopsies for histologic confirmation. Also excludes mechanical complications (strictures, fistulae) and cuffitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible endoscope is passed through the anal canal into the pouch. The procedure takes 15-20 minutes; bowel preparation with clear liquids and a purgative is required. Sedation is optional but often used. Biopsies are taken painlessly.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Validated composite scoring tool integrating clinical symptoms, endoscopy findings, and histology. A score of 7 or above confirms pouchitis; 4-6 is '疑似' pouchitis; below 4 is '疑似' pouch.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Completed by the clinician based on history, endoscopic findings, and biopsy results. Provides an objective measure of disease activity for baseline and follow-up comparison.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A non-invasive biomarker of intestinal inflammation. Elevated fecal calprotectin (>200 µg/g) supports pouchitis diagnosis and is used to monitor response to treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single stool sample collected in a standard container. Results return within 1-3 days. Can be used for ongoing monitoring without repeated endoscopy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Appropriate for patients with a short pouch or when full pouchoscopy is not available. Assesses the anal transitional zone and distal pouch; may miss proximal pouch disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A shorter scope inserted 10-15 cm. Less preparation required; sedation rarely needed. Useful for cuffitis assessment and surveillance in stable patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used when a peripouch abscess, fistula, or surgical complication is suspected and endoscopy is insufficient to diagnose the complication.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CT with oral contrast or MRI with rectal contrast. Takes 30-60 minutes. Neither requires sedation. MRI is preferred in younger patients to avoid radiation.

diagnosticTests.test: C-reactive protein (CRP) and complete blood count
diagnosticTests.purpose: Supportive tests for systemic inflammation. CRP is elevated in moderate-severe pouchitis; anemia or leukocytosis may be present in severe episodes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results return within hours.

treatmentOverview: Pouchitis management follows a step-up approach based on severity and frequency of episodes. For first-line and intermittent pouchitis episodes, a 2-week course of oral metronidazole 500 to 750 mg twice daily is the standard initial treatment, with clinical response in 80 to 90 percent of patients within days of starting therapy. Alternatively, oral ciprofloxacin 500 to 1000 mg twice daily for 2 weeks is equally effective with better tolerability, preferred first-line in many contemporary clinics. For recurrent or chronic antibiotic-dependent pouchitis, the antibiotic choice is guided by prior response, stool patterns, and antibiotic sensitivity testing. Budesonide 9 mg daily (oral or rectal) is an effective corticosteroid option for antibiotic-refractory cases. For chronic pouchitis unresponsive to antibiotics and steroids, immunomodulators (azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine) or anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab) are used, though evidence is limited to case series. VSL number 3 high-dose multi-strain probiotic formulation has demonstrated efficacy in preventing pouchitis and maintaining remission in antibiotic-responsive disease, worth discussing for appropriate patients. Surgical revision or pouch excision is reserved for severe refractory cases with persistent incontinence, severely reduced quality of life, or dysplasia or cancer. Dietary modification including low-residue diet during flares and lactose avoidance supports medical management. Long-term antibiotic maintenance therapy may be considered for patients with frequent relapses who are not candidates for or are refractory to immunomodulator therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Ciprofloxacin (500 mg twice daily for 14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antibiotic for acute pouchitis. Highly effective against gram-negative and anaerobic bacteria that are thought to drive pouch inflammation. Rapid onset of symptom relief within 3-5 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom resolution in approximately 80-90% of acute pouchitis episodes; endoscopic and histologic remission in 60-70%.

medicalTreatments.name: Metronidazole (400-500 mg three times daily for 14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line anaerobic antibiotic for pouchitis. Particularly active against Bacteroides and Clostridium species. Slightly lower efficacy than ciprofloxacin but still approximately 80% effective in acute episodes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom resolution in approximately 75-80% of acute pouchitis episodes. Metronidazole is also used in combination with ciprofloxacin for severe or recurrent episodes.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral budesonide (9 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: A locally acting corticosteroid with high anti-inflammatory potency and low systemic bioavailability. Used for chronic antibiotic-refractory pouchitis when antibiotics fail.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response in approximately 50-60% of refractory pouchitis patients; endoscopic remission in approximately 30-40%. Tapering is required to avoid adrenal suppression.

medicalTreatments.name: VSL#3 probiotic (450 billion CFU daily)
medicalTreatments.description: A high-potency probiotic formulation containing 8 strains of bacteria (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, Streptococcus). Used for maintenance therapy in chronic antibiotic-responsive pouchitis and prevention of recurrence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomized trial data (Gionchetti 2000) showed that VSL#3 maintains remission in approximately 85% of chronic pouchitis patients over 9 months versus 3% in placebo.

medicalTreatments.name: Azathioprine / 6-mercaptopurine
medicalTreatments.description: Immunomodulators used for chronic antibiotic-refractory pouchitis. Slow onset (8-12 weeks to full effect). Useful for maintaining remission in patients who respond to anti-TNF or steroids.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response in approximately 40-50% of chronic refractory pouchitis; primarily used as steroid-sparing maintenance rather than induction monotherapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-TNF biologics (infliximab 5 mg/kg, adalimumab 160/80/40 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-TNF monoclonal antibodies used for severe refractory pouchitis unresponsive to antibiotics and steroids. Target TNF-alpha, the key cytokine driving the inflammatory response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Open-label series show clinical response in 40-60% of refractory pouchitis patients; endoscopic remission in 30-40%. Response rates are lower than in Crohn's disease, reflecting the distinct pathophysiology of pouchitis.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic balloon dilation of pouch-anal strictures
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic treatment for fibrotic strictures at the pouch-anal anastomosis. A balloon catheter is inflated under direct vision to stretch and widen the stricture. Short-term success rates are approximately 60-80%, though recurrence of stricturing is common.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Immediate technical success in >90% of cases; recurrence requiring repeat dilation in 30-50% within 12 months.

surgicalOptions.name: Pouch revision (redundancy reduction, advancement)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical revision of the pouch to address structural problems — large pouch size, redundant loops causing stool stasis, or outlet obstruction. Considered when medical therapy fails and structural abnormality is identified.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom improvement in approximately 60-70% of selected cases; significant morbidity and risk of pouch failure if revision is incomplete.

surgicalOptions.name: Pouch excision with permanent ileostomy
surgicalOptions.description: The definitive surgical treatment for severe, refractory pouchitis when all medical and revisional options have been exhausted. The pouch is removed and the patient is left with a permanent end ileostomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive resolution of pouchitis symptoms in nearly 100% of cases. Life-changing surgery with significant impact on body image and quality of life — considered only after exhaustive medical management.

alternativeTreatments.name: Budesonide enemas (2 mg/100 mL nightly)
alternativeTreatments.description: Topical corticosteroid delivered directly to the pouch via enema. Useful for distal pouch and cuff inflammation. Lower systemic absorption than oral budesonide.

alternativeTreatments.name: 5-aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA) enemas or oral formulations
alternativeTreatments.description: Topical mesalamine enemas for pouch inflammation; less effective than antibiotics for acute pouchitis but useful for maintenance in combination with probiotics.

whySeeSpecialist: A gastroenterologist with expertise in inflammatory bowel disease and pouch disorders should manage pouchitis — ideally at an IBD center with dedicated pouchoscopy experience. Referral is warranted when acute pouchitis fails to respond to a standard 14-day antibiotic course, when symptoms recur frequently (3 or more times per year), when there is bloody stool suggesting possible Crohn's disease of the pouch, or when surgical options are being considered.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a gastroenterologist who performs pouchoscopy regularly and has a dedicated IBD/pouch surveillance program. Ask about the number of pouch patients managed and whether they use the PDAI scoring system for baseline assessment. A colorectal surgeon with pouch experience should be involved early if structural complications (strictures, fistulae) are suspected.
preventionStrategies: Probiotic supplementation with VSL#3 immediately post-pouch surgery — randomized trial data suggests it reduces the incidence of pouchitis in the first year
Smoking cessation in non-smokers is not relevant — non-smokers actually have lower pouchitis risk
Regular pouchoscopy surveillance (annually after year 3 post-surgery) — detects subclinical inflammation and allows early treatment
Fecal calprotectin monitoring every 6-12 months post-surgery — elevated values precede symptomatic pouchitis in many patients, allowing pre-emptive treatment
Avoid unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotics post-surgery unless clearly indicated — these alter the pouch microbiome and may increase pouchitis risk
lifestyleModifications: Dietary modification — identify and avoid trigger foods (often dairy, high-fiber foods, caffeine, alcohol, spicy foods) during pouchitis episodes; a low-residue diet during acute flares reduces stool frequency
Hydration — increased stool frequency leads to fluid loss; adequate water intake is essential to prevent dehydration
Pouch irrigation (evacuation regimen) — some patients benefit from daily pouch irrigation with warm water to reduce bacterial overgrowth and maintain pouch cleanliness
Pelvic floor physical therapy — for patients with high stool frequency from functional outlet dysfunction, pelvic floor retraining can improve evacuation efficiency
Stress management — psychological stress can worsen pouch symptoms; mindfulness, cognitive behavioral therapy, and adequate sleep are beneficial
Iron supplementation — iron deficiency anemia from chronic blood loss in active pouchitis requires iron replacement (oral or IV depending on severity)

recommended: Low-residue diet during acute pouchitis flares — reduces stool frequency by limiting bowel-stimulation; white rice, bananas, toast, lean protein are well-tolerated
Probiotic-rich foods (yogurt with live cultures, kefir, fermented vegetables) — may support pouch microbiome health
Small, frequent meals — distributes bowel stimulation throughout the day rather than overwhelming the pouch with three large meals
Soluble fiber supplements (psyllium) when in remission — can improve stool consistency and reduce urgency by absorbing water
Clear soups and bone broth — easily digestible, provides electrolytes, and is gentle on the pouch during flares
avoid: Dairy products (lactose) — lactose intolerance is common post-colectomy and worsens pouchitis symptoms (gas, cramping, diarrhea)
High-fiber and high-residue foods during acute flares — nuts, seeds, raw vegetables, popcorn, corn can inflame and obstruct an already inflamed pouch
Caffeine and carbonated drinks — stimulate intestinal motility and worsen urgency and frequency
Alcohol — irritates the pouch mucosa and worsens diarrhea; beer is particularly problematic due to carbonation plus alcohol
Spicy foods and high-fat foods — can worsen cramping, urgency, and diarrhea in an inflamed pouch
Artificial sweeteners (sorbitol, mannitol) — osmotic diarrhea trigger in patients with bile acid malabsorption

exerciseGuidelines: During acute pouchitis episodes, exercise should be reduced — high-intensity activity can exacerbate diarrhea and urgency. Low-impact activities such as walking, yoga, and light stretching are appropriate. During remission, normal exercise including running, cycling, and swimming is encouraged. Core-strengthening exercises (planks, gentle abdominal work) may require modification early after pouch surgery but are appropriate in the longer term.
dailyManagement: Track stool frequency, urgency, and any blood in a daily diary — patterns often emerge that help identify triggers and guide treatment
Take antibiotics exactly as prescribed — complete the full 14-day course even if symptoms improve within a few days
Flush pouch with warm water after defecation during acute episodes — reduces bacterial load and soothes inflamed mucosa
Use barrier cream on perianal skin — prevents and treats perianal skin irritation from frequent stools
Attend annual pouchoscopy surveillance even when feeling well — subclinical inflammation can be detected and treated before symptoms develop
prognosis: The vast majority of pouchitis episodes are acute and responsive to antibiotics — approximately 80-90 percent of first episodes resolve with a 14-day course. However, approximately 30-40 percent of patients will experience recurrent episodes, and approximately 10-15 percent develop chronic antibiotic-refractory pouchitis, which is the most challenging form to manage. With appropriate escalation from antibiotics to steroids to immunomodulators and anti-TNF therapy, most chronic pouchitis patients achieve reasonable symptom control, though complete resolution is often elusive. Quality of life in chronic refractory pouchitis is significantly impaired — comparable to Crohn's disease disability scores. In the most severe refractory cases, pouch excision is the definitive solution. The overall pouch retention rate at 20 years is approximately 90 percent, meaning most patients keep their pouch with appropriate management. The key determinant of prognosis is whether the underlying diagnosis is truly ulcerative colitis (favorable) or Crohn's disease (less favorable).
recoveryTimeline: Acute pouchitis episodes respond to antibiotic therapy within 3-7 days — most patients report substantial symptom improvement by day 5 of a 14-day course. Full endoscopic and histologic resolution lags behind symptom resolution by approximately 1-2 weeks. Chronic antibiotic-refractory pouchitis that requires escalation to steroids or biologics may take 4-8 weeks to achieve meaningful symptom control. Pouch revision surgery requires 4-8 weeks of recovery before normal activities can resume. Pouch excision surgery requires a longer recovery of 2-3 months.
complications: Chronic antibiotic-refractory pouchitis — the most serious long-term complication; leads to progressive quality of life impairment, malnutrition, and may ultimately require pouch excision
Pouch failure and excision — approximately 5-10 percent of IPAA pouches are eventually excised, most commonly for refractory pouchitis or misdiagnosed Crohn's disease

Pouch strictures and outlet obstruction — fibrotic narrowing at the pouch-anal anastomosis causes difficulty evacuating, incomplete emptying, and abdominal cramping; requires endoscopic dilation or surgical revision
Pouch fistulae and perianal disease — uncommon in pure pouchitis but raises concern for Crohn's disease of the pouch; requires MRI assessment and complex surgical management
Malnutrition and weight loss in chronic refractory pouchitis — chronic diarrhea, malabsorption, and reduced intake can lead to significant weight loss and hypoalbuminemia

supportResources.name: Crohn's & Colitis Foundation — Pouch Surgery and Pouchitis
supportResources.description: Patient education resources on IPAA surgery, pouchitis management, and living with a pouch.

supportResources.name: International Organisation for IBD (IOIBD) — Pouchitis Consensus Guidelines
supportResources.description: International expert consensus on pouchitis diagnosis and treatment — the most authoritative clinical guideline.

supportResources.name: United Ostomy Associations of America
supportResources.description: Support resources for patients facing or living with pouch excision and permanent ileostomy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ankylosing Spondylitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Crohn's disease involving the pouch is the most important differential to exclude — it is often refractory to standard pouchitis therapy, requires anti-TNF biologics or immunomodulators from the outset, and has a higher pouch failure rate. Key distinguishing features include skip lesions, deep linear ulcers, fistulae, and non-caseating granulomas on biopsy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cuffitis involves inflammation of the retained rectal mucosal cuff above the anastomosis, not the pouch itself. Presents with rectal bleeding and urgency rather than increased stool frequency. Treated with topical 5-ASA or corticosteroids — antibiotics are ineffective.

relatedConditions.name: Ulcerative Colitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Pouchitis is a complication of the surgical treatment of UC. The underlying inflammatory bowel disease pathophysiology that caused UC (immune dysregulation, mucosal inflammation) drives pouchitis in the surgically constructed pouch.

relatedConditions.name: Ankylosing Spondylitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Spondyloarthropathy (peripheral arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis) is the most common extraintestinal manifestation of UC and can persist or develop after IPAA surgery, particularly in association with active pouchitis flares.

faqs.question: What is pouchitis?
faqs.answer: Pouchitis is inflammation of an ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA) — a surgically constructed pouch made from the small intestine and connected to the anal canal after colectomy for ulcerative colitis. It is the most common long-term complication of IPAA surgery, affecting 40-50 percent of patients at some point.

faqs.question: How is pouchitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Pouchitis is diagnosed using the Pouchitis Disease Activity Index (PDAI), which combines clinical symptoms, endoscopic findings, and histology. Pouchoscopy — passing a flexible endoscope into the pouch — is the gold standard, showing characteristic inflammation (edema, friability, erosions). Biopsies confirm the diagnosis.

faqs.question: What is the best antibiotic for pouchitis?
faqs.answer: Ciprofloxacin (500 mg twice daily for 14 days) is the most effective single antibiotic for acute pouchitis. Metronidazole (400-500 mg three times daily for 14 days) is an alternative. Combination therapy (both antibiotics together) is used for severe or recurrent episodes.

faqs.question: Is pouchitis curable?
faqs.answer: Acute pouchitis episodes are curable with a 14-day course of antibiotics — approximately 80-90 percent of first episodes resolve completely. However, approximately 30-40 percent of patients experience recurrent episodes, and 10-15 percent develop chronic antibiotic-refractory pouchitis that is not curable and requires ongoing management.

faqs.question: What is chronic antibiotic-refractory pouchitis?
faqs.answer: This is pouchitis that does not respond to standard antibiotic therapy (ciprofloxacin and/or metronidazole). It requires escalation to oral budesonide, immunomodulators, or anti-TNF biologics. It is the most challenging form and significantly impairs quality of life.

faqs.question: Can pouchitis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Probiotic supplementation with VSL#3 immediately post-surgery has been shown in randomized trials to reduce the incidence of pouchitis in the first year. Regular pouchoscopy surveillance (annually after year 3) and fecal calprotectin monitoring detect subclinical inflammation for early treatment.

faqs.question: What diet is recommended for pouchitis?
faqs.answer: During acute pouchitis flares, a low-residue diet (white rice, bananas, toast, lean protein) reduces stool frequency. Avoid dairy (lactose intolerance is common after colectomy), caffeine, alcohol, spicy foods, and high-fiber foods. During remission, gradually reintroduce fiber and dairy.

faqs.question: What is the difference between pouchitis and Crohn's disease of the pouch?
faqs.answer: Pouchitis is inflammation of the pouch with no skip lesions, diffuse symmetric inflammation, and response to antibiotics. Crohn's disease of the pouch has skip lesions, deep linear ulcers, fistulae, and is refractory to standard pouchitis treatment. Distinguishing them is critical because treatment differs substantially.

faqs.question: What is cuffitis?
faqs.answer: Cuffitis is inflammation of the retained rectal mucosal cuff — a small ring of rectal tissue left above the pouch-anal anastomosis. It presents with rectal bleeding and urgency rather than increased stool frequency. Treated with topical 5-ASA or corticosteroids, not antibiotics. It is often confused with pouchitis.

faqs.question: How common is pouchitis after IPAA surgery?
faqs.answer: Approximately 40-50 percent of IPAA patients experience at least one pouchitis episode during their lifetime. The median time to first episode is 1-3 years after surgery, but episodes can occur at any time, even decades later.

faqs.question: Does pouchitis ever go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Mild pouchitis episodes may resolve spontaneously, but most episodes require antibiotic treatment for timely resolution. Untreated pouchitis typically persists and worsens over days to weeks.

faqs.question: What probiotic is used for pouchitis?
faqs.answer: VSL#3 is the most evidence-based probiotic for pouchitis — a high-potency formulation with 450 billion CFU of 8 bacterial strains. Randomized trial data (Gionchetti 2000) showed it maintains remission in approximately 85% of chronic pouchitis patients over 9 months versus 3% on placebo.

faqs.question: Can pouchitis lead to pouch failure?
faqs.answer: Yes — approximately 5-10 percent of IPAA pouches are eventually excised, most commonly for refractory pouchitis or misdiagnosed Crohn's disease. Most patients keep their pouch with appropriate management. The key is early, accurate diagnosis and timely escalation of treatment.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with an IPAA pouch?
faqs.answer: Life expectancy after IPAA surgery is essentially normal. Pouchitis itself is not life-threatening. The main risks are pouch failure (requiring excision and permanent ileostomy) in approximately 5-10 percent, and the small risk of pouch cancer in patients with long-standing refractory inflammation.

faqs.question: What is fecal calprotectin in pouchitis?
faqs.answer: Fecal calprotectin is a protein released by neutrophils at sites of intestinal inflammation. It is markedly elevated in pouchitis (often >200 µg/g, versus <50 µg/g in a healthy pouch) and is used as a non-invasive biomarker to diagnose and monitor pouchitis without repeated endoscopy.

faqs.question: What causes pouchitis?
faqs.answer: The exact cause is not fully understood, but current evidence points to a combination of fecal stasis causing bacterial overgrowth, dysregulated mucosal immune response (Th1/Th17 polarization), genetic susceptibility (NOD2 variants), and loss of colonic short-chain fatty acid production after colectomy.

faqs.question: Can you get pouchitis if you had surgery for FAP (not UC)?
faqs.answer: No — pouchitis does not occur in patients who had IPAA for familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP), because their colon is normal and not inflamed before surgery. Pouchitis only develops in the context of the inflammatory bowel that preceded the surgery (ulcerative colitis or, rarely, Crohn's disease).

faqs.question: What is the Pouchitis Disease Activity Index (PDAI)?
faqs.answer: The PDAI is a validated scoring tool for pouchitis that assigns points across three domains: clinical symptoms (0-6 points), endoscopic findings (0-6 points), and histology (0-6 points). A total score of 7 or above confirms pouchitis; 4-6 is '疑似' (possible); below 4 is '疑似' (unlikely).

faqs.question: When should I consider pouch excision?
faqs.answer: Pouch excision with permanent ileostomy is considered when all medical options (antibiotics, steroids, immunomodulators, anti-TNF biologics) have failed, when structural complications (severe strictures, fistulae) cannot be managed endoscopically or with revision surgery, and when quality of life is severely impaired. It is a last resort.

faqs.question: What are the extraintestinal manifestations of pouchitis?
faqs.answer: Pouchitis can be associated with extraintestinal manifestations similar to those seen in inflammatory bowel disease: joint pain (arthralgias, peripheral arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis), skin lesions (erythema nodosum, pyoderma gangrenosum), and eye inflammation (uveitis, episcleritis). These often improve with treatment of the underlying pouch inflammation.

metaTitle: Pouchitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Pouchitis: inflammation of the ileal J-pouch after surgery for ulcerative colitis. Symptoms, antibiotic treatment, and prevention reviewed by our medical board.

images.altText: Anatomical illustration showing the colon, rectum, and anal canal — the regions relevant to ileal pouch-anal anastomosis construction and pouchitis
images.caption: Ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA) is constructed by removing the colon and rectum, creating a J-shaped pouch from the terminal ileum, and connecting it to the anal canal.
